problem_summary: 2020 MCM Problem A (continuous): Scotland's herring and mackerel fisheries face warming North Atlantic seas. The team had to (1) predict where the two species relocate over the next 50 years as ocean temperature changes, (2) determine best/worst/most-likely times until small fishing companies operating from current ports can no longer harvest, (3) recommend whether and how those companies should change operations, and (4) assess how migration of fish into other nations' territorial waters affects the recommendations. The team built a chain of three coupled models — an ocean thermal-equilibrium model feeding a logistic fish-growth model feeding a fishery economic-benefit model — to answer all four questions end to end.
models_used: Oceanic thermal equilibrium model: a physics-based ocean surface heat-budget Qw = Qs - Qb +/- Qe +/- Qh combining solar radiant energy Qs = Qs0(1-0.7C)(1-As)sinH (solar constant 344 W/m^2, cloud factor C~0.5, sea albedo As=7%, mean solar altitude H=38.5 deg), effective back-radiation Qb=A-B (long-wave 90%Qs0 minus atmospheric return 50%Qs0), evaporation/condensation latent heat Qe=L*k*(e0-ez)*W via Dietrich empirical formula, and atmosphere-ocean sensible heat Qh=10%Qw

Localized heat-balance extension adding horizontal advection Qa = -Cp*rho*v*(dT/dn) (temperature-gradient advection by ocean currents), with vertical transport Qz assumed negligible; total Qt then converted to temperature change via Qt = c*m*deltaT using c=4096 J/kg.C and estimated shallow-water mass m=3.255e14 kg, giving deltaT = Qt/(cm)

Migration prediction by temperature-niche matching: cumulating annual deltaT (~+2.6 C over 50 years) onto a 2019 SST heat map, then locating the (longitude, latitude) cells matching each species' preferred band (herring 8.8-9.8 C, mackerel 8.86-9.6 C) to produce decade-by-decade migration coordinates

Fish school growth model: a logistic-type ODE dN_i/dt = (r_i - f_i - d_i)*N_i with spawning rate r coupled to temperature change via dr/dt = -a*(dT/dt) (faster warming -> lower local spawning rate), parameterized separately for herring (f=0.27, r=0.36, d=0.09, a=0.1) and mackerel (f=0.22, r=0.41, d=0.19, a=0.2), with a 10% of initial-stock threshold defining 'no harvest'

Micro-element (forward-Euler) iterative solver to integrate the coupled growth ODE and detect the threshold-crossing year under best (min deltaT=0.011), worst (max deltaT=0.087) and most-likely temperature-change scenarios

Fishery company economic-benefit model: profit S(t) = sum N_i(t)*f_0i*p_i comparing four strategies — no change, relocating assets (fraction a, split origin/relocated revenue Sb+Sa), using refrigerated new boats (fraction b, full-stock access N_0i), and catching a new species (averaged price/stock/fishing-rate, scaled by c) — calibrated to a 2019 reference profit S0 = GBP108.19M

Territorial-water modification: an 'influence parameter' alpha (fraction of fish NOT migrated into foreign waters) inserted as (1-alpha) into the relocation and new-boat revenue terms to discount unreachable catch
key_techniques: Built a physics-first forward-prediction chain rather than a regression/extrapolation: derived future SST from a closed-form heat budget instead of curve-fitting historical temperature, which gives a mechanistic, defensible 2.6 C / 50-yr projection

Model coupling as the spine of the whole paper — thermal model output (deltaT) becomes an input to the growth model (via dr/dt = -a*dT/dt) whose output (N_i(t)) becomes an input to the economic model; a single Figure-2 flowchart makes this dependency explicit
Scenario bracketing (best/worst/most-likely) driven by the min/max of the predicted temperature-change rate, turning Q2's 'best and worst case' wording directly into model parameter choices
Threshold abstraction: defining 'almost no fish to catch' operationally as N_i(t) < 10% of initial stock, converting a vague business question into a concrete first-passage-time computation

Reusing averaged parameters (mean price, mean stock, mean fishing rate scaled by c) to model a hypothetical 'new species' for which no data exists — a clean way to answer a counterfactual sub-question

Single-parameter perturbation knob design: scalars a, b, c (strategy intensities) and alpha (foreign-water effect) each collapse a strategy to one tunable number, making the economic comparison and Q4 modification trivial to compute and plot
Tool split: Python (folium HeatMap + CircleMarker/PolyLine) for geospatial migration maps, MATLAB for the economic-curve sweeps over a/b/c/alpha

Sensitivity analysis framed as elasticity: perturbing dT/dt by +/-18% and showing the threshold-year response stays within ~15% (and <3% in the worst-case branch), reported as relative change rates to argue stability
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective the paper wins on completeness and coherence rather than on any single sophisticated technique. It answers all four sub-questions with a clean, traceable model chain (thermal -> growth -> economics) so nothing feels bolted on, and the Figure-2 solving-strategy flowchart lets a judge grasp the whole architecture in seconds. The thermal-equilibrium model is the standout differentiator: most teams extrapolated SST from data, but this team derived it from a real ocean heat budget (solar, back-radiation, latent, sensible, advective terms), which reads as genuine domain effort and yields a concrete, physically grounded 2.6 C number. It then converts every fuzzy MCM phrase into something computable — 'best/worst case' becomes min/max deltaT, 'no harvest' becomes a 10% threshold first-passage time, 'change operations' becomes four parameterized profit curves, 'other country's waters' becomes the single parameter alpha. Each sub-question ends with a crisp, decision-useful answer (year-31 most-likely collapse; relocate for long-term, new boats for short-term, new species highest but consumer-risky). The two-page Hook Line and Sinker magazine letter translates the math into plain-language advice with specific years and ranked recommendations, exactly the audience-aware communication judges reward, and the sensitivity section closes the loop with elasticity evidence of stability."
section_highlights: "Strong structural moves worth copying: (1) a 'Our work' narrative section plus a Figure-2 model-dependency flowchart placed before any math, so the reader sees the whole pipeline first; (2) six explicitly justified simplifying assumptions, each with a one-line physical/economic rationale and a citation, rather than a bare bullet list; (3) a symbol table up front; (4) every model presented as analyze -> assumptions -> equations -> algorithm -> solution-of-problem-N, so each question has a visibly self-contained answer; (5) the economic model written as four parallel sub-sections with the consistent identity that setting the strategy parameter to 0 recovers the 'no change' baseline — a built-in sanity check the authors call out; (6) a dedicated audience-facing magazine article with To/From/Date header and ranked, plain-language recommendations; (7) a sensitivity section that reports relative change rates and explicitly bounds them ('<15%', '<3%') to make the stability claim concrete; (8) full Python and MATLAB appendices for reproducibility."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Migration prediction is the weakest link: the decade-by-decade (lon,lat) coordinates in Table 2 jump around erratically (e.g., herring at (24 W,58 N) in 2050) with no transport/dispersal dynamics — fish are simply placed wherever the SST grid matches the niche band, so the 'routes' are not modeled, only snapshots of temperature-matching cells

Numerical bug in the stated algorithm: Step 5 updates N_i(t+1) = N_i(t) + delta r_i (adds the spawning-rate increment) instead of + delta N_i; the intended stock update appears mis-transcribed, undermining trust in the iteration as written

Internal inconsistency in reported parameters: the abstract/text say spawning rate 0.30 for herring while Table 3 lists r=0.36, and d_i = r_0i - f_i is asserted as an equilibrium identity yet the tabulated d values don't satisfy it cleanly

The 'best/worst case' temperature rates (0.011 vs 0.087) are taken as the min and max of the same 50-year deltaT curve, conflating temporal variation within one scenario with genuinely different climate scenarios — there is no IPCC-style emissions pathway underpinning worst-case

Heavy reliance on point-estimate constants (cloud factor C=0.5, single annual mean solar altitude, fixed wind speed, constant fish prices via Assumption 6, stable total stock via Assumption 5) with no uncertainty propagation; the lone sensitivity analysis perturbs only dT/dt, not the many heat-budget or economic parameters
Economic model assumes fish prices are constant despite a collapsing supply, which is economically implausible (scarcity would raise prices) and conveniently simplifies the profit comparison

The OCR/markdown of the source has corrupted several heat-map tables and figure data, but even allowing for that, several profit-curve figures show all strategy curves nearly identical, suggesting the parameter sweeps had limited visual separation
reusable_patterns: The three-stage coupled-model spine: environmental/physical driver -> population/state dynamics -> economic/decision layer, with each stage's output as the next stage's input and one flowchart showing the coupling. Reusable for any 'climate/resource affects a population affects a business' prompt.

Derive-don't-extrapolate: when a quantity (temperature, demand, growth) has known governing physics or mechanics, build the closed-form budget/balance equation instead of regressing history; it reads as deeper modeling to judges.

Operationalize vague rubric words into computable quantities: map 'best/worst case' to parameter min/max, 'can no longer X' to a threshold first-passage time, 'should they change' to parameterized strategy curves, 'enters another country' to a single (1-alpha) discount factor.

Single-knob strategy parameterization: represent each candidate decision by one scalar intensity (a, b, c) such that 0 recovers the do-nothing baseline; then a parameter sweep + one plot answers 'which strategy and how much'.

Logistic growth with an environmentally-coupled rate: dN/dt=(r-f-d)N where r is driven by an external forcing (dr/dt = -a*dT/dt) is a compact, defensible template for any harvested-population-under-stress question.
Built-in baseline sanity check: design the model so a parameter=0 case must equal the null/no-action case, and state this explicitly as validation.
Audience-deliverable as a first-class section: a short, ranked, plain-language memo (To/From/Date) restating the quantitative findings for the stakeholder named in the prompt.
Sensitivity reported as bounded elasticity: perturb the most influential input by +/-X% and show the key output's relative change stays under a stated bound, then call the model 'stable'.
